dailyJudgementSnippet: OpenAI got the clearest provider-specific boost on the front page: the GPT-5.4 Pro math story sparked unusually impressed discussion, with commenters treating it as evidence of genuinely novel capability rather than just another model release [E1].
winnerExplanation: OpenAI won because the strongest direct sentiment signal of the day was concentrated in one high-engagement story about GPT-5.4 Pro solving a frontier math open problem, and the top comments amplified that result instead of dismissing it [E1]. Google Gemini also had a constructive Show HN mention, but it was lighter and more mixed because discussion quickly shifted to surveillance concerns rather than sustained praise of the model itself [E2].

openai.judgementSnippet: GPT-5.4 Pro solving a frontier math open problem drove a concentrated wave of impressed reactions, including commenters saying the result changed their view of whether LLMs can produce genuinely novel work [E1].

anthropic.judgementSnippet: Claude Code was singled out as a daily productivity booster in one thread, but another used Anthropic’s failed C-compiler experiment as a concrete example of why current agents still do not reliably converge even with strong tests [E3][E4].

google_gemini.judgementSnippet: A Show HN project built on Gemini’s native video embeddings drew interest and concrete use-case ideas, though one of the biggest replies focused on searchable-camera privacy risks more than on Gemini quality itself [E2].

evidence.summary: The title centers on GPT-5.4 Pro solving a frontier math open problem, and top comments describe the result as changing their mind about whether LLMs can produce novel solutions.

Gemini’s native video embeddings powered a Show HN demo that commenters called cool and useful for search/editing workflows, though a top reply warned about panopticon-style surveillance enabled by this kind of capability.
In the AI-fatigue discussion, a commenter explicitly called Claude Code amazing and said it makes them much more efficient at work every day.
A comment on the Hegel/property-testing thread cites Anthropic’s C-compiler experiment as evidence that current AI agents can still fail badly even when tests are strong.
